figures.gloss: The supreme of the Upperworld — lord of the sacred mountain, the sun, and the hornbill, dwelling above the world on the cosmic peak. He is the male half of the godhead: the bright, dry, ascendant principle, ruler of the diurnal sky and of all that rises. From his side come the headwaters of order, and his emblem-bird the hornbill perches at the top of the Tree of Life.
figures.facet: A sky-father who is explicitly and only ONE HALF — Mahatala is not a complete high god but the male pole of a godhead that is incomplete without its watery female counterpart below. The Ngaju supreme is a hemisphere, not a whole; sovereignty here is shared by structural necessity, never solitary.
figures.provenance: Hans Schärer, *Ngaju Religion: The Conception of God among a South Borneo People* (Dutch 1946, Eng. trans. 1963); the name appears as Mahatala / Lahatala, an Islamic-tinged form (cf. Allāh Taʿālā) overlaid on an indigenous Upperworld lord. Cf. Waldemar Stöhr on Borneo religions.

figures.gloss: The watersnake-goddess of the Underworld and the underwater — mistress of the river, the deep, and the moon, coiled beneath the world as Mahatala reigns above it. She is the female half of the godhead: the dark, wet, descendant principle, mother of the waters and of all that sinks and is reborn from below. Her serpent body girds the lower world as the hornbill crowns the upper.
figures.facet: The cosmic serpent here is not the slain dragon nor the encircler-at-the-edge but a co-sovereign DEITY — half of God — ruling her hemisphere with exactly the dignity of the sky-lord above. The deep is not chaos to be conquered; it is the divine feminine completing the divine masculine, watersnake answering hornbill.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963), the watersnake-goddess of the Underworld; Jata is paired with Mahatala throughout Ngaju cosmology and ritual. Schärer renders the pair as the two manifestations of a single bisexual godhead.

figures.gloss: The godhead in its wholeness: not Mahatala alone nor Jata alone but their UNION — a single two-in-one, bisexual totality of which hornbill-sun-mountain-male and watersnake-moon-river-female are the inseparable halves. Neither is complete or even fully real without the other; divinity itself is the bound dyad, and every sacred act re-enacts their joining.
figures.facet: A supreme deity that is structurally a MARRIAGE — the absolute is not One that later divides, but Two that are originally and eternally one-as-two. The Ngaju do not climb from many to one; they hold that the one IS the perfect complementary pair, and the cosmos exists in the tension and reunion of its halves.
figures.provenance: Schärer's central thesis (*Ngaju Religion*, 1963): the godhead is a total, bisexual unity manifest as the Upperworld/Underworld dyad. This duality-in-unity organises Ngaju myth, ritual, social moieties, and village layout alike; the interpretation is Schärer's classic structural reading.

figures.gloss: The Tree of Life rising between the worlds, joining Upperworld and Underworld along the cosmic axis. Upon it the two primal hornbills — the bird of Mahatala and the bird of Jata — meet and fight, and from the destruction and shattering of the tree the ordered cosmos, the goods of culture, and the first human beings come forth. Its renewal is the renewal of the world; it is carved as the spear-bladed golden tree of Ngaju art.
figures.facet: An axis-mundi whose meaning is CONFLICT-AND-RENEWAL, not serene centre — the world is born from the violent collision of the two godhead-halves upon the tree and the tree's own destruction. Creation is the price of the primal pair meeting; the tree must shatter for the cosmos to stand, and is renewed in every Tiwah.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963), the Batang Garing and the battle of the two hornbills; the Tree of Life is the master-symbol of Ngaju iconography. Schärer reads the hornbill-combat as the union/opposition of the godhead's halves generating the world.

figures.gloss: The great watersnake-dragon of the Underworld and the deep waters — the serpentine body of the lower cosmos, intimately bound to Jata as her form and domain. The Tambon coils through the underworld rivers as the counterpart to the upperworld hornbill, and figures on the sacred ship and the ritual ground as the lower half of the world's two-bodied frame.
figures.facet: The serpent as one of the cosmos's two STRUCTURAL BODIES — paired explicitly with the hornbill, Tambon is not a monster but the lower world's emblem-beast, the Underworld's answer to the Upperworld bird. The Ngaju animal cosmos is a single creature in two halves: bird above, snake below.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963); the Tambon/Naga watersnake of the Underworld, paired with the Tingang hornbill as the upper/lower emblems on ritual objects (the sacred ship, the bone-house). The bird/snake dyad pervades Ngaju material culture.

figures.gloss: The hornbill — emblem-bird of the Upperworld and of Mahatala, the sky-side made feathered. The Tingang crowns the Tree of Life and the prow and stern of sacred objects, the casqued great bird whose flight is the upper cosmos in motion. It is the avian body of the world's bright half, set against the watersnake of the deep.
figures.facet: A sky-bird whose whole significance is to be HALF OF A PAIR — the hornbill means nothing alone but everything as the upper term opposite the watersnake. Where other traditions' eagles or thunderbirds reign supreme, the Tingang is one wing of a two-bodied cosmos, complete only when the snake is named beneath it.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963); the Tingang hornbill as Upperworld emblem and bird of Mahatala, paired with the Tambon watersnake. The hornbill is the pre-eminent sacred bird across Borneo Dayak art (cf. Stöhr).

figures.gloss: The heavenly spirit-beings who mediate between gods and humans — and the sacred ritual language, basa Sangiang, in which the priests address the Upperworld. The Sangiang carry prayers up and blessings down, conduct the souls of the dead, and speak through the balian and basir priests in their esoteric tongue. They are the living traffic between the worlds, without whom the dyad could not communicate across its gulf.
figures.facet: Mediation given a LANGUAGE OF ITS OWN — the Sangiang are not only psychopomps but the speakers and the speech itself, a whole sacred dialect that exists to bridge Upperworld and Underworld. The seam between the two halves is staffed by spirits AND voiced in a tongue reserved for crossing it.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963); basa Sangiang is the priestly ritual language of the Ngaju, distinct from everyday Ngaju. The Sangiang mediate in the Tiwah and other rites; documented also in later Ngaju ethnography and in studies of Kaharingan religion.

figures.gloss: The great secondary mortuary feast — the climactic rite in which the bones of the dead are gathered and the soul is at last ferried up the golden ship of the Sangiang to the prosperous village of the Upperworld. Sacrifices, the Tree of Life renewed, and days of priestly chant in basa Sangiang convey the dead from the lingering, dangerous state of the newly-deceased to a finished ancestor at home above.
figures.facet: Death completed in TWO STAGES and crowned by a soul-ship — the Ngaju dead are not done at burial but only at the Tiwah, when the golden vessel of the heavenly mediators carries them up. The cosmos's two halves meet one last time over each person: the body returns to the lower world, the soul sails to the upper.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963); and Peter Metcalf & Richard Huntington, with Metcalf's *A Borneo Journey into Death* (1982) on Borneo secondary treatment of the dead — the double-funeral pattern Robert Hertz first theorised. The golden soul-ship is a core Ngaju funerary image.

figures.gloss: The first human beings — lowered from the Upperworld, or brought forth at the Tree of Life from the events of the primal hornbill-combat — the ancestral pair from whom the Ngaju descend. Their origin ties humankind directly to the godhead's halves and to the cosmic tree: people are the issue of the upper and lower worlds' meeting, born of the same union that made the cosmos.
figures.facet: Humanity as a DESCENDED, not an autochthonous, line — the first pair come down from above or out of the tree's destruction, so that to be human is to carry the upper world's lineage into the lower. The ancestor here is the cosmos's dyad made flesh and set walking on the middle earth.
figures.provenance: Schärer, *Ngaju Religion* (1963), the origin of humans from the Tree of Life and the descent of the first ancestors from the Upperworld; variants are recorded across Ngaju myth. Reconstructed motif — the exact narrative of the first pair varies by source and is flagged as such.

figures.gloss: The Ngaju ritual specialists who actually chant basa Sangiang and conduct the Tiwah and healing rites — balian (priestesses) and basir (male or intersex priests, often cross-dressing in ritual function).
figures.facet: They are the rite-holders the whole system depends on: the Sangiang mediate between worlds in myth, but it is the balian and basir who speak the sacred tongue and perform the mediation in practice.
figures.provenance: Hans Schärer, 'Ngaju Religion: The Conception of God among a South Borneo People' (1946; Eng. trans. 1963).

figures.gloss: The raised, carved bone-repository house where the cleaned remains of the dead are installed after the Tiwah completes their passage — the visible monument of Ngaju religion across Kalimantan villages.
figures.facet: It is the Tiwah's destination structure made permanent architecture: the rite ends, but the sandung stands afterward as the community's ongoing address for its ancestors.
figures.provenance: Hans Schärer, 'Ngaju Religion' (1963); Peter Metcalf, 'A Borneo Journey into Death' (1982).

figures.gloss: The 'prosperous village,' the Ngaju Upperworld home of the finished dead to which the Sangiang's golden ship carries the soul at the close of the Tiwah.
figures.facet: It names the destination the Tiwah entry only gestures at — the Upperworld is not an abstraction but a specific, prosperous settlement the dead actually arrive in.
figures.provenance: Hans Schärer, 'Ngaju Religion' (1963), on the Tiwah's Upperworld destination.

figures.gloss: The sacred book of Hindu Kaharingan, compiled in the 20th century as the religion sought Indonesian state recognition, recording the Ranying Hatalla cosmogony and ritual chants in codified form.
figures.facet: It is the living tradition's own answer to an ethnographer's monograph: where the roster otherwise rests entirely on Schärer's 1946 fieldwork, the Panaturan is Kaharingan writing its own scripture for the modern state.
figures.provenance: Kaharingan institutional publications recognized alongside Indonesia's official religions; discussed in later ethnography of Kaharingan revival (cf. references in Waldemar Stöhr on Borneo religions).

figures.gloss: Vampiric witch-spirits of Ngaju belief — humans whose heads detach by night to prey on the living, the feared and maleficent class the ordered dyadic cosmos otherwise leaves unnamed.
figures.facet: They are the roster's missing dark remainder: a cosmos built entirely of paired, dignified complements (sky/water, bird/snake) still needs a feared inversion, and this is it.
figures.provenance: Hans Schärer, 'Ngaju Religion' (1963); later Ngaju ethnography on witch-belief.

edges.note: The two halves of the one godhead — hornbill-sun-male above bound to watersnake-moon-female below; neither complete without the other.
Mahatala is the male, upperworld pole of the single bisexual godhead.
Jata is the female, underworld pole of the single bisexual godhead.
The cosmos's two emblem-bodies — hornbill above answered by watersnake below — paired on every sacred object.
The Tambon watersnake is Jata's serpent-body and domain, the form of the underworld she rules.
The Tingang hornbill is Mahatala's emblem-bird, the upperworld sky made feathered.
The two primal hornbills meet, fight, and shatter the Tree of Life, generating the cosmos.
The Sangiang and their golden ship ferry the soul upward in the Tiwah; the mediators staff the rite.
Two co-defining complementary principles — bright/dry/male and dark/wet/female — whose union is the whole; a structural parallel to the Dao's paired powers.
Heaven-and-Earth as the bound creative/receptive pair — Mahatala's sky-mountain and Jata's water-deep rhyme with Qian and Kun.
A single bisexual godhead held as a male-female unity — the Ngaju two-in-one parallels the Nahua Lord/Lady of Duality.
The absolute as an originally-paired complementarity rather than a One that later divides.
A great watersnake-goddess of the deep waters and fertility — a structural rhyme with the Australian Rainbow Serpent, sex and locality differing.

A loose parallel — both are great serpent-beings of cosmic standing, but Quetzalcoatl is sky-feathered culture-bringer where Tambon is the underworld-water body; resemblance is the serpent-emblem, not the role.
The deep and the river personified as the divine feminine — Jata is the mother-of-waters as the watersnake-goddess of the underworld sea.
A cosmic tree joining the worlds along the central axis — the Batang Garing rhymes with the Maya ceiba as world-axis.
World-tree spanning upper and lower realms; the Ngaju tree's destruction-and-renewal parallels Yggdrasil's role as the axis bearing the cosmos.
The world-axis as the meeting of the realms — the Batang Garing and the cosmic mountain both centre the layered cosmos.
The ordered world and the first humans arise from the destruction of the primal tree in the hornbills' combat — a making-from-a-shattered-primal-thing.
A loose parallel — the soul ferried by a sacred ship to a prosperous afterworld rhymes with the Egyptian boat-of-the-dead and the barque of Osiris, though the cosmologies are unrelated.
The golden soul-ship of the Sangiang conducts the dead up to the Upperworld village — a structural psychopomp rite.
A loose parallel only — Mahatala is a bright sky-lord, but he is one half of a dyad rather than the autonomous PIE sky-father; the resemblance is the sky-association, not the structure.
The descended first pair as the ancestral source of the people — humankind as the issue of the cosmos's two halves.
The balian and basir are the human specialists who speak basa Sangiang and perform the mediation the Sangiang embody in myth.
The carved bone-house is the Tiwah's destination structure, receiving the cleansed remains once the rite completes the dead's passage.
The golden ship of the Sangiang ferries the soul to this named Upperworld village at the Tiwah's close.
The 20th-century Kaharingan scripture codifies the Mahatala-Jata cosmogony for state recognition.
Ritual specialists whose office crosses ordinary gender categories, chanting or channeling in a register reserved for addressing the spirits.

A documented name-loan, not shared theology: 'Lahatala' is an Islamic-tinged form of Allah Ta'ala laid over the indigenous Upperworld lord, who structurally remains only the male half of Ngaju religion's bisexual godhead-dyad.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Ngaju, a Dayak people of the great rivers of South Borneo (Central Kalimantan, Indonesia); its organized modern form, recognized by the Indonesian state since 1980, is called Kaharingan. Its distinctive claim is a single godhead that is intrinsically two-in-one — the hornbill sky-god Mahatala above and the watersnake goddess Jata below — whose union is the whole of divinity.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is tiered: an Upperworld (Mahatala; hornbill, sun, mountain) and an Underworld and underwater realm (Jata; watersnake, moon, river), joined by the middle earth of humans and by the Tree of Life (Batang Garing). The world arises from the meeting and combat of the two primal hornbills upon the tree and its shattering. Death is completed in two stages — after primary burial the soul lingers dangerously until the great secondary mortuary feast (Tiwah) ferries it up a golden ship, guided by the Sangiang spirit-mediators, to the prosperous village of the Upperworld.
dossier.practice: Priests — the balian and basir — conduct the rites, chanting in an esoteric sacred language, basa Sangiang. The pivotal ceremony is the Tiwah, the secondary funeral at which the bones are gathered, buffalo and pigs sacrificed, and the bone-house (sandung) raised; the same upper/lower dual symbolism orders village layout, social groups, and the carved hornbill-and-watersnake imagery of Ngaju art.
dossier.canon: An oral corpus of cosmogonic and mortuary chants in basa Sangiang, with no indigenous scripture; the classic record is Hans Schärer's 'Ngaju Religion' (Dutch 1946; English 1963), and organized Kaharingan has since compiled a modern holy book, the Panaturan.
dossier.reflects: The godhead as an eternal marriage — the sacred conceived not as a One that later divides but as an original complementary dyad (sky-hornbill-male and water-snake-female), so that cosmos, society, and every rite re-enact the union of two halves.
sources: Hans Schärer, 'Ngaju Religion: The Conception of God among a South Borneo People' (1963)
Anne Schiller, 'Small Sacrifices: Religious Change and Cultural Identity among the Ngaju of Indonesia' (1997)
Robert Hertz, 'Death and the Right Hand' (1907; trans. 1960)
